coredata.dc:title: A label-free, rapid, sensitive and selective technique for detection of Fe2+ using SERRS with 2,2′-bipyridine as a probe
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Guangda
Li, Na
Sun, Ye
Gao, Ce
Ma, Liping
Song, Peng
Xia, Lixin
coredata.dc:description: We have developed a label-free, concise, time-efficient, ultrasensitive, and selective strategy for the determination of Fe2+ ion based on surface-enhanced resonance Raman scattering (SERRS) spectroscopy. There was no Raman signal because of the weak adsorption of 2,2′-bipyridine (Bpy) on silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). Upon the addition of Fe2+, Bpy quickly formed [Fe(Bpy)3]2+ complexes with Fe2+ and produced an intense Raman signal through inducing the aggregation of AgNPs, which provided a theoretical foundation for quantitative analysis of Fe2+. The proposed SERRS platform displayed a wide linear range from 10–11 to 10–7 M with an unprecedented detection limit of 5.73 pM. The whole process can be completed in 3 min and provided a new universal route for practical detection in various water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface-enhanced resonance Raman scattering
2,2′-bipyridine
Silver nanoparticles
Fe2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SERRS measurement of Bpy in the absence and presence of Fe2+
Selectivity and reproducibility of the SERRS platform

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis spectra of AgNPs (a) and AgNPs mixed with Bpy (b) and AgNPs mixed with Bpy in the presence of 10–5 M Fe2+ (c).
SEM image of AgNPs (A) and TEM image of AgNPs (B) and TEM image of AgNPs mixed with Bpy in the presence of 10–5 M Fe2+ (C).
SERS spectra of Bpy with AgNPs (a) and Raman spectra of Bpy (b) and SERS spectra of Bpy with AgNPs (5 mW) (c) and SERRS spectra of Bpy with AgNPs in the presence of 10–5 M Fe2+ (d).

Optimization of the concentration of Bpy (A), the value of pH (B), the volume of AgNPs (C), the reaction time (D). The Raman intensity represented the peak at 1487 cm−1. The error bars represented the standard deviations based on three independent measurements at least.
SERRS intensity at 1487 cm−1 band against the logarithm of concentration of Fe2+ ranging from 10–11 to 10–5 M.

(A) The linear plot of the Raman intensity at 1487 cm−1 against the logarithm of Fe2+ concentration. (B) The three-dimensional SERRS spectra of AgNPs mixed with Bpy in the presence of different Fe2+ concentrations.
Bar chart (A) based on the Raman intensity at 1487 cm−1 and SERRS spectra (B) of AgNPs mixed with Bpy in the presence of 10–5 M Fe2+ and 10–4 M other metal ions.
Schematic illustration of the sensing mechanism for Fe2+.
Determination of Fe2+ in practical samples via the proposed method and ICP-MS.
Comparisons of the performance of several analytical methods for detecting Fe2+.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The 500 nm-130 um should go to the next row

head.ce:title: A label-free, rapid, sensitive and selective technique for detection of Fe2+ using SERRS with 2,2′-bipyridine as a probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Physics, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 2,2′-Bipyridine quickly formed [Fe(Bpy)3]2+ complexes with Fe2+ and produced an intense Raman signal at 1487 cm−1 which provided a theoretical foundation for quantitative analysis of Fe2+.

We have developed a label-free, concise, time-efficient, ultrasensitive, and selective strategy for the determination of Fe2+ ion based on surface-enhanced resonance Raman scattering (SERRS) spectroscopy. There was no Raman signal because of the weak adsorption of 2,2′-bipyridine (Bpy) on silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). Upon the addition of Fe2+, Bpy quickly formed [Fe(Bpy)3]2+ complexes with Fe2+ and produced an intense Raman signal through inducing the aggregation of AgNPs, which provided a theoretical foundation for quantitative analysis of Fe2+. The proposed SERRS platform displayed a wide linear range from 10–11 to 10–7 M with an unprecedented detection limit of 5.73 pM. The whole process can be completed in 3 min and provided a new universal route for practical detection in various water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, highly sensitive, selective, and quantitative analyses of trace metal ions [1], biomarkers [2-7], and gaseous pollutant [8] are necessary for the organisms because they are closely related to human health and the ecological environment [9]. Among all of the metal ions, iron ions, the most common transition metal ions in the body, they have two forms (ferric/ferrous), and play an indispensable role in numerous biological processes such as respiration, oxygen transport and DNA synthesis [10,11]. In particular, Fe2+ is important in the production of hemoglobin and monitoring its level which could contribute to studying its role in biological processes [12]. However, the concentration of iron ions must be controlled within a reasonable range: elevated concentrations will lead to a series of diseases like cancer, Alzheimer’s disease, Parkinson’s disease and so on [13]; low concentrations of iron induces anemia and reduces immunity [14]. The maximum allowable concentration in drinking water is 0.3 mg/L (about 5.4 μM) according to the World Health Organization recommendation [15].

To date, several conventional analytical methods have been applied to determine Fe2+/Fe3+ including fluorescent analysis[16-18], chemiluminescence [19], colorimetry [20,21], electrochemical [22-25], atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [26], chromatographic [27], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [28] and so on. However, these methods always have some defects such as tedious pretreatment, complex synthesis process, low detection accuracy and long processing time. Hence, it is necessary to develop a facile, ultrasensitive, and speed method for the detection of Fe2+.

Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) spectroscopy has been regarded as a promising new technique for the determination of trace metal ions because of its special fingerprint identification capability, high sensitivity, rapid detection ability and simple operation in recent years [29,30]. For example, Yang et al. proposed a novel and stable SERS sensor for Fe3+ based on silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) capped with inositol hexaphosphate (IP6) [31]. Afterwards, their group put forward a gold nanoparticles/ITO (AuNPs/ITO) film decorated with 4-pyridinethiol to detect Hg2+ ions [32]. Docherty et al. determinated metal ions by SERS technique using 2,2′-Bipyridine (Bpy) complexes [33]. A colorimetric/fluorescent/SERS triple-mode sensor for Cu2+ ions detection based on binaphthalene (BAMH)-modified Au nanorods (GNRs@BAMH) was built by Deng’s group [34]. In our group, Sun et al. designed a novel strategy to recognize Ag+ by surface plasmon-catalyzed oxidation of 4-aminodiphenyl disulfide to p,p′-dimercaptoazobenzene [35]. However, so far, we have found almost no one who has made use of SERRS technique to detect Fe2+.

Resonance Raman scattering (RRS) occurs when the excitation laser wavelength falls near the molecular electron absorption peak [36]. However, surface-enhanced resonance Raman scattering (SERRS), which is a combination of SERS and RRS, can increase the Raman cross section by 10–14 orders of magnitude [37]. SERRS greatly improves the detection limit of traditional Raman scattering, allowing it to be measured from molecules that would otherwise be silent in the spectrum [38]. Therefore, SERRS technique has recently gained applications in bacterial cells [39], DNA detection [40], reaction mechanism monitoring [41], biomedical diagnostics [42] and provides a theoretical basis for the detection of ultra-low concentration substances [43].

2,2′-Bipyridine, a common ligand, can form complexes with lots of metals in different forms such as mono-, bis- or tris-complexes based on the ligand and metal ions concentration. When Bpy is in excess, Fe2+ react with it to form tris-complex. Scheme 1 shows the reaction process between Bpy and Fe2+. Bpy crystals normally present a trans-configuration and a C 2h symmetry, while they also exists in cis-configuration with a C 2v symmetry especially when forming complexes with metal ions [44,45].

In this work, we present a novel strategy for the rapid detection of Fe2+ based on SERRS in which available Bpy served as a label-free probe. When Bpy was mixed with AgNPs, SERS signals were not generated at low laser intensity (0.5 mW) due to the weak adsorption of nitrogen atoms on the Ag surface, which is because of the higher Bpy–Ag0 charge transfer complexation energy and the longer N–Ag bond distance [46]. Once Fe2+ ions were added, Bpy could rapidly form [Fe(Bpy)3]2+ complexes with Fe2+ (within 3 min) through space configuration torsion and produce a strong SERRS signal of the complex at 1487 cm−1 on account of its maximum absorption wavelength at 522 nm which is extremely closer to the excitation laser wavelength (532 nm). As the concentration of Fe2+ increased, so did the concentration of the [Fe(Bpy)3]2+ complexes, thus enhancing the signal intensity. The proposed method demonstrated a good linear range from 10–11 to 10–7 M with a low detection limit of 5.73 pM, which is six orders of magnitude lower than the concentration required by the WHO. What′s more, this approach has been successfully applied to the detection of Fe2+ ions in water samples and sheds a new light on practical detection.

The synthesis method of [Fe(Bpy)3]2+ was based on previous literature [47]. Briefly, 2,2′-bipyridine (79.6 mg, 0.51 mmol) and FeSO4·7H2O (47.3 mg, 0.17 mmol) were added to the MeOH/CH2Cl2 solution (10 mL) and stirred at room temperature for 30 min under N2. The solution was distilled under reduced pressure and dried under vacuum. The synthesized compound was directly used for SERRS tests without purification.

Various concentrations of fresh Fe2+ solution were obtained by continuous dilution of the standard solution by deionized water before testing. Bpy solutions were dissolved with ethanol. In a typical experiment, 100 μL Bpy solution and 100 μL Fe2+ solution were mixed with 125 μL AgNPs solution for 3 min at room temperature. The mixture was drawn into a capillary and tested using a confocal Raman micro-spectrometer (Renishaw, UK). The laser power was 0.5 mW except for special illustration.

The river water was from the Hun River, Shenyang, Liaoning, China. The industrial wastewater was from Liaoshen Industrial Group Co., Ltd., Shenyang, Liaoning, China. The apple juice was purchased from the local supermarket. The river water and industrial wastewater samples were filtered using 0.45 μm membrane and then were spiked with Fe2+ (1, 10 and 100 nM). The apple juice samples were spiked with different concentrations of Fe2+ (1, 10 and 100 nM) and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min. Then the supernatants were used for testing.

The reaction process and sensing mechanism are properly described in Scheme 2 . When Bpy was added to AgNPs, SERS signals were not generated at low laser intensity (0.5 mW) due to the weak adsorption of nitrogen atoms on the Ag surface. Upon the addition of Fe2+, Bpy can rapidly form [Fe(Bpy)3]2+ complexes with Fe2+ (within 3 min) through space configuration torsion and produce a strong SERRS signal of the complex at 1487 cm−1 which is attributed to a C = N stretch and C–H in-plane deformation [48]. When Fe2+ ions are coordinated with Bpy, the distance between the Bpy is reduced, simultaneously inducing the aggregation of AgNPs, which causes the appearance of a strong SERRS signal. Accordingly, a concise sensing platform to detect Fe2+ has been established based on the characteristic peak at 1487 cm−1.

To investigate further the reason for the signal enhancement, we analyzed the UV–vis spectra. The maximum absorption wavelength of AgNPs is about 422 nm (Fig. 1 a). It can be seen by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) that the diameter of AgNPs is about 30 nm and the distribution is relatively dispersed (Fig. 2 A and B). When AgNPs were modified with Bpy, its maximum absorption wavelength was almost unchanged, indicating that Bpy had little effect on AgNPs (Fig. 1b). In addition, the maximum absorption peak of Bpy appeared at 280 nm. When 10–3 M Fe2+ was added to the system, new absorption peaks appeared at 520 nm and 490 nm, which meant that new material was formed (Figure S1). Furthermore, its maximum absorption wavelength was 520 nm, similar to the laser excitation wavelength (532 nm), which produced a resonance effect to enhance the signal intensity. In order to prove further that the generated new substance was [Fe(Bpy)3]2+, we synthesized its standard product and compared them. The obtained UV–vis and SERRS (Figure S2) diagrams were basically identical. However, the absorbance at 520 nm reduced with the decrease of Fe2+ concentration. When the concentration of Fe2+ dropped to 10–5 M, the absorption peaks at 490 nm and 520 nm disappeared, which also showed that the traditional UV–vis method had certain limitations. At the same time, the absorbance of AgNPs at 422 nm was weakened and the peak broadened, which was due to the aggregation of AgNPs via adding the Fe2+ (Fig. 1c). To verify our conjecture, the TEM image is presented in Fig. 2C. It can be clearly seen that after the addition of Fe2+, the AgNPs produced a certain amount of aggregation. This is due to the coordination action of Fe2+ ions and Bpy, which reduces the distance between AgNPs and generates more “hot spots”. To certify further that not only is the resonance effect causing the enhancement, but the electromagnetic enhancement also plays a more important role, the Resonance Raman (RR) spectrum and SERRS spectrum are shown in Figure S3. It is clear that the SERRS spectrum had a stronger Raman signal, suggesting that the electromagnetic enhancement was dominant in the enhancement mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERRS measurement of Bpy in the absence and presence of Fe2+
ce:section.ce:para: We measured the Raman and SERS spectra of Bpy and SERRS spectra of Bpy in the presence of 10–5 M Fe2+ first. Because of the weak adsorption of Bpy on the Ag surface, the SERS signal was not generated when the laser power was 0.5 mW (Fig. 3 a). The SERS spectrum of Bpy is shown in Fig. 3c when the laser power was increased by ten times (5 mW). The characteristic vibration peaks of Bpy can be clearly observed. Comparing the Raman spectrum (Fig. 3b) and SERS spectrum of Bpy, the characteristic peaks of the two spectra were basically similar and their assignments are listed in Table S1 [48]. To research the influence of adding Fe2+, we contrasted the SERS spectra under different powers (if the laser power of Fig. 3d is raised, the peak intensity will exceed the range). After Fe2+ was added to the system, the SERRS spectrum changed significantly. There was an increase of peak intensity at 660 cm−1, 1560 cm−1 and 1603 cm−1, a decrease of peak intensity at 766 cm−1, and the appearance of new peaks at 1022 cm−1 and 1319 cm−1, all of which indicated that the spatial structure of Bpy transformed from trans-form to cis-form [45]. It is a remarkable fact that the band at 1487 cm−1, attributed to a C = N stretch and C–H in-plane deformation, enhanced dramatically compared with other peaks. This might be the result of the stretching vibration of the aromatic ring affected by the combination of the lone electron pairs on Fe2+ and the nitrogen atoms on Bpy [33]. Comparing Fig. 3a with 3d, in the absence of Fe2+, there was no SERRS signal. When Fe2+ was added, a strong SERRS signal was generated which provided a theoretical foundation for quantitative analysis of Fe2+.

For best probe performance, we varied the experimental factors that might affect the results, such as the concentration of Bpy, the pH of the solution, the volume of silver colloid, the reaction time between substrate and Fe2+, and obtained the optimal reaction parameters finally.

It is known that the concentration of the probe will affect the reaction. So, we varied the concentration of Bpy from 10–3 to 10–6 M. As shown in Fig. 4 A, 10–3 M is the optimal probe concentration. The results at high and low concentrations are not very befitting. The pH of the system might affect the form of Fe2+ ions. By our measurement, the pH of the Bpy solution was around 8. The SERRS signal significantly decreased when we raised the pH of the solution which was due to the possibility that ferrous hydroxide was formed. We also found that the results under acidic conditions were not ideal either (Figure S4), probably because the Bpy was protonated under acidic conditions and restrained the formation of compound. Hence, we selected 8 as the best pH for the system. Generally speaking, the volume of AgNPs with the same concentration also affects the SERRS performance. Therefore, it is necessary to select a suitable silver colloid volume. As can be seen from Fig. 4C, 125 μL was the optimal experimental condition. Finally, the SERRS performance of [Fe(Bpy)3]2+ complex was examined at different reaction time. SERRS signals became stronger and stronger as the mixing time increased. Until the reaction reached 3 min, the signal strength was nearly unchanged, which proved that the reaction was completed on the whole. So far, we have finished the optimization of experimental conditions.

We conducted SERRS measurement under the optimal reaction conditions to investigate whether the proposed method can quantitatively detect Fe2+. The results are exhibited in Fig. 5 . It is obvious that the characteristic signal peak at 1487 cm−1 gradually increased by varying the concentration of Fe2+ from 10–11 to 10–5 M. In Fig. 6 A, a satisfactory linear relationship was presented between the Raman intensity at 1487 cm−1 and the logarithm of Fe2+ concentration ranging from 10–11 to 10–7 M (R 2 = 0.9995). The equation of the regression line was Y = 20130.8351 + 1717.2493log(X) (Y represents the Raman intensity at 1487 cm−1 and X represents the concentration of Fe2+). In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were calculated to be 5.73 and 19.01 pM, respectively (the LOD and LOQ were determined by 3σ/k and 10σ/k, respectively, where σ is the standard deviation of the background and k is the slope of the calibration curve), which was improved by 6–7 orders of magnitude compared with traditional analysis measures such as UV–vis spectroscopy. Fig. 6B more intuitively reflects that the method has a favorable linear relationship.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and reproducibility of the SERRS platform
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the proposed method, a series of controlled experimental results were obtained between Fe2+ and other metal ions that may produce interference such as K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Co2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Bi3+, Pb2+ and Fe3+. Each experiment was conducted individually under the optimal reaction conditions. The concentration of Fe2+ was set as 10–5 M, while the concentration of other metal ions was ten times higher than that of Fe2+. As can be clearly seen in Fig. 7 , the reaction of other metal ions with Bpy produced almost no significant signal enhancement and had little interference to Fe2+. In fact, some metal ions can also form complexes with Bpy, but this method is still able to specifically detect Fe2+ because the other metal ions complexes cannot produce resonance enhancement effect (Figure S5). In addition, the reproducibility of the SERRS sensor was also evaluated. We measured 10 different points with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.2%, which exhibited a excellent reproducibility of the SERRS platform (Figure S6).

In order to validate that the designed method has practical application ability, we chose river water, industrial wastewater and apple juice as the detection object. The results are presented in Table 1 . The satisfactory recoveries ranged from 94.68% to 107.19% and RSD was less than 5%, which indicated the proposed sensor could be effectively applied to the detection of Fe2+ in practical samples. At the same time, we also employed traditional analysis methods such as inductively coupled plasma–mass spectrometry (ICP–MS) for detection of Fe2+ and obtained similar results, which also proved that SERRS had high accuracy and preferential sensitivity in determination in an intricate water environment. The SERRS spectra of analysis of Fe2+ in water samples can be observed in Figure S7–S9. To highlight the advantages of this approach, some assays for detecting Fe2+ are listed in Table 2 . It can be seen that the proposed technique had more favorable advantages over other methods in terms of linear range, limit of detection and detection time.

In conclusion, a facile, time-efficient, inexpensive, ultrasensitive and ultrahigh sensitivity approach for trace detection of Fe2+ combined with SERRS technology based on complexation between Bpy and Fe2+ was designed. Under the optimal reaction conditions, the Raman intensity and Fe2+ concentration revealed a satisfactory linearity in the range from 10–11 to 10–7 M with a low LOD of 5.73 pM and LOQ of 19.01 pM, respectively. In addition, the SERRS protocol has been successfully applied to detect Fe2+ in river water, industrial wastewater and apple juice without complicated pretreatment, which has great potential to be a competitive candidate for the rapid Fe2+ detection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the LiaoNing Revitalization Talents Program (Grant No. XLYC1807162), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21671089 and 11974152), the Shenyang High-level Innovative Talents Program (RC200565).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.128741.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
